description: Annual grain crop that thrives in Texas summers. Plant in blocks (not rows) for pollination. A quick-win annual that produces in 70-90 days. Works as a trellis for beans (Three Sisters guild).

One of the best understory fruit trees for Texas. Blooms November–January (unusual winter bloom), ripens fruit March–May. Evergreen, ornamental, and incredibly productive. Tolerates part shade and alkaline soil. A must-have for Texas food forests.
toxicityWarning: Seeds contain cyanogenic glycosides — do not eat seeds

description: Classic Texas stone fruit. Choose low-chill varieties (150-400 chill hours) for Central and South Texas. Plant in well-drained soil; does not tolerate wet feet. One of the highest-yielding backyard fruit trees when properly sited.
toxicityWarning: Leaves, bark, and seeds contain cyanogenic compounds — do not eat

description: Requires careful variety selection for Texas. Choose low-chill varieties (Anna, Dorsett Golden, Ein Shemer) for Central/South Texas. Higher-chill varieties work in North Texas Hill Country. Plant two varieties for cross-pollination.

Fire-blight resistant varieties (Kieffer, Moonglow, Orient) perform best in Texas. Very reliable producer once established. Tolerates heavier soils than most fruit trees. Long-lived (50+ years). Excellent high-canopy understory tree.

Lower chill-hour requirement than European pears. Crisp, apple-textured fruit. Very disease resistant. Excellent choice for Central Texas where European pears struggle. Self-fruitful varieties available.

The most cold-hardy lemon for Texas. Produces fruit nearly year-round. Thin-skinned, sweet, prolific. Protect below 20°F. In Central Texas grow in containers or against south-facing walls. Excellent in South Texas and Gulf Coast in ground.

Most cold-hardy mandarin for Texas. Ripens October–December (earlier than other citrus). Very productive, easy to peel, seedless. Tolerates brief cold snaps to 15°F. Excellent choice for all but the coldest Texas locations.

Key lime. Very productive in South Texas. More cold-sensitive than lemons — protect below 28°F. Small tree, dense canopy. Produces heavily with minimal care in appropriate zones. Bloom attracts pollinators spring through fall.

Texas's most famous citrus, bred in the Rio Grande Valley. Exceptional sweetness and color. Needs longer growing season — best in South Texas and Gulf Coast. In Central Texas, grow against south-facing masonry walls for reflected heat.

Requires acidic soil (pH 4.5–5.5) — a challenge in alkaline Central Texas but doable with raised beds and soil acidification. Rabbiteye varieties (Tifblue, Brightwell) perform best in Texas. Highbush varieties need more chill hours. Plant 2+ varieties for cross-pollination.

The 'miracle tree' for Texas food forests. Fastest-growing edible tree in warm climates — can reach 15ft in one season. Leaves, pods, seeds, and flowers all edible and highly nutritious. Excellent nitrogen fixer and dynamic accumulator. Dies back to roots in cold winters but regrows vigorously from roots.

Grows vigorously in Texas Zones 8b+. Dies back to ground below 28°F but regrows from rhizomes. Produces suckers that create dense clumps — perfect for windbreak and microclimate creation. Fruit production requires consistently warm winters (reliable in zones 9+).

Tree spinach — one of the most productive perennial vegetables for Texas. Leaves must be cooked (boiled 5+ min) before eating to neutralize HCN — cooked greens taste like spinach. Grows 6–12ft, extremely drought-tolerant once established, produces leaves year-round in warm climates. Spreads via cuttings.
toxicityWarning: Raw leaves are toxic — must be boiled for 5+ minutes before eating

description: Heat-loving annual nitrogen fixer that thrives in Texas summers when other legumes fail. Fixes significant nitrogen while producing edible peas. Fast-growing, drought-tolerant, and improves soil for subsequent plantings. Varieties: Black-Eyed Pea, Crowder Pea, Purple Hull. Plant spring and fall.

One of the most productive nitrogen-fixing trees in the world. Fast-growing shrub/small tree producing massive amounts of biomass for mulch. Pods and young leaves are edible. Thrives in Texas heat and alkaline soils. Can be coppiced repeatedly. Use as a chop-and-drop mulch producer near fruit trees.
toxicityWarning: Mimosine in seeds and leaves can cause hair loss in non-ruminant animals — avoid feeding livestock in large quantities

description: Ancient grain and leafy green thriving in Texas heat. Leaves are edible raw or cooked (similar to spinach). Seeds are a protein-rich grain. Self-seeds prolifically. Heat and drought tolerant. Excellent bee forage. Use as a chop-and-drop mulch producer once seed is set.

Essential Texas salsa ingredient. Extremely productive annual. Grows 3–4ft and sprawls — use a cage. Plant at least 2 for pollination. Prolific self-seeder — you'll have volunteers every year after the first. Excellent companion under taller plants; handles part shade.
toxicityWarning: Remove husk before eating — husk is mildly toxic

description: Cool-season herb for fall and spring in Texas (bolts in summer heat). Excellent pest-confuser and insectary plant — flowers attract predatory wasps. Let it bolt and set seed; the seeds (coriander) are also edible. Self-seeds reliably. Plant densely under taller summer plants for succession.

Excellent insectary and pest-confuser plant. Flowers attract parasitic wasps and beneficial insects. Host plant for Black Swallowtail butterfly caterpillars. Cool-season annual in Texas — plant fall through spring. Self-seeds. Companion to brassicas and fruit trees for pest management.

Vigorous perennial groundcover and pest-confuser. Aromatic oils deter aphids and some beetles. Spreads aggressively via runners — plant in containers or use as an intentional groundcover. Prefers moist soil and part shade. Excellent companion near aphid-prone plants.

Slightly more heat-tolerant than peppermint. Good summer performer in part shade. Excellent groundcover under fruit trees — suppresses weeds, confuses pests with scent, and provides edible harvest. Spreads via runners. Contains rosmarinic acid that deters some soil pathogens.

German chamomile — excellent insectary plant whose flowers attract beneficial insects. Called 'the physician of the garden' for its beneficial effects on neighboring plants. Cool-season annual in Texas; plant fall through spring. Self-seeds profusely. Use as companion near brassicas, onions, and fruit trees.

One of the best insectary plants for the food forest. Blue star-shaped flowers bloom prolifically and attract bees continuously. Dynamic accumulator (high in potassium, calcium, silicon). Excellent companion for tomatoes and strawberries. Self-seeds aggressively — treat as a welcome volunteer.

Texas native understory tree producing tart red berries beloved for jelly-making. Blooms January–March when little else is flowering — critical early nectar source for pollinators. Tolerates wet, low areas where other fruit trees struggle. Beautiful ornamental with fall foliage. One of Texas's most underutilized native fruit trees.

Extremely productive and disease-resistant fruit tree for Texas. Requires very few chill hours. Large fragrant yellow fruits used for jam, paste, and cider. Blooms late (avoids frost damage). Tolerates clay and alkaline soils. Often overlooked but one of the best-adapted fruit trees for Texas conditions.

Southern staple that is among the most productive cool-season greens for Texas. Extremely cold-hardy (survives hard freezes). Plant in fall for winter production. Works under fruit trees in partial shade during summer. Can grow as perennial in Zones 9+ with careful management. Bee flowers if allowed to bolt.

The summer spinach solution for Texas — unlike true spinach, thrives in heat. Vining plant that climbs fences and trellises. Leaves taste like spinach. Perennial in Zones 9+, annual elsewhere (self-seeds). High in iron, calcium, and vitamins. Produces all summer when true greens fail.

Compact relative of tomatillo producing sweet, papery-husked fruits with vanilla-pineapple flavor. Self-seeds prolifically — plant once and harvest for years. Thrives in Texas heat. Fruits ripen when husks turn tan and fall from plant. Excellent under taller plants; handles part shade.
toxicityWarning: Remove papery husk before eating; green/unripe fruits may cause digestive upset

description: Texas food forest star. Ornamental shrubby annual producing tart crimson calyxes used for tea, jam, and agua fresca. Thrives in Texas summer heat. Blooms attract pollinators. Grows 5-7ft in one season. Easy from seed each spring. One of the most beautiful and productive additions to a food forest.

Fast-growing tropical producing fruit in 6-12 months from seed. Perennial in zones 9b+, treated as annual elsewhere. Dies below 32°F but can be grown as summer annual in Central Texas with impressive results. Seeds, leaves, and fruit all edible. Needs well-drained soil — does not tolerate wet feet.

Hardy Mexican varieties (Fantastic, Lula, Mexicola) survive to 22°F. Best suited for Zone 9b+ in Texas. Needs excellent drainage. Large productive canopy tree when established. Plant two trees (one Type A and one Type B) for best fruit set. Very rewarding in South Texas and Gulf Coast.

Perennial allium that returns reliably every year in Texas. Purple ball-shaped flowers are excellent bee forage. Pest-confusing aroma deters aphids and carrot fly. Excellent border plant and groundcover companion under fruit trees. Divide clumps every 2-3 years. Extremely low-maintenance.

Culinary sage — woody perennial herb with strong pest-confusing aroma. Attracts bumblebees. Excellent companion near brassicas and carrots. Drought-tolerant once established. Dies back in hard freezes but usually regrows. Use under fruit trees as pest deterrent.

Powerhouse pest-confuser and insectary plant. Root exudates suppress soil nematodes. Aromatic foliage deters aphids, whitefly, and some beetles. Flowers attract beneficial insects and pollinators. French marigolds (small) are especially effective for nematode suppression. African marigolds (tall) are excellent bee forage.

Cool-season annual that does double duty as pest trap and insectary plant. Aphids prefer nasturtiums over most food crops — plant as a sacrificial trap crop near vulnerable plants. Flowers and leaves are edible and peppery. Excellent groundcover under fruit trees in fall and spring.

Fast-growing native shrub with massive umbrella flower clusters — premier insectary plant. Berries used for syrup, wine, and medicine. Grows 6-12ft in one season. Tolerates wet areas. Can be coppiced for biomass. Roots spread via suckers. One of the most wildlife-valuable shrubs for Texas food forests.
toxicityWarning: Raw berries and uncooked plant parts can cause nausea — always cook berries before consuming

description: One of the most heat and drought-tolerant fruit shrubs for Texas. Produces abundantly with minimal water once established. Beautiful orange flowers attract hummingbirds. Tolerates alkaline soil exceptionally well. Long-lived (100+ years). Excellent choice for hot, dry Texas conditions.

Exceptional performer in Texas — one of the most heat and drought-tolerant fruit trees available. Produces dates-like fruits that taste like apple-honey when fresh. Very long-lived (100+ years). Tolerates alkaline and poor soils where other fruit trees struggle. Almost no pest or disease problems.

Smooth-skinned peach relative requiring the same low-chill varieties as peaches in Texas (Suncoast, Sunbest, Sunraycer for Central Texas). More susceptible to brown rot than peaches in humid East Texas — best in drier Central and West Texas. Same care requirements as peach.

Japanese plum varieties (Methley, Bruce, Morris, Byrongold) perform best in Texas. Low chill requirements (150-400 hours). Very productive when properly sited. Cross-pollination improves fruit set — plant two varieties. Highly drought-tolerant once established in well-drained soil.

Extremely productive, fast-growing Texas performer. Produces abundant fruit with zero effort. Fruits are the most productive berry per square foot of any tree in Texas. Excellent wildlife plant — birds will flock to it. Red mulberry is native to Texas. White and hybrid varieties are more drought-tolerant. Excellent nurse tree.
toxicityWarning: Unripe fruits and white sap may cause hallucinations and digestive upset — only eat ripe fruits

description: Premier nitrogen-fixing shrub that also produces tart-sweet edible berries. One of the few nitrogen fixers that produces significant food while also building soil. Extremely drought-tolerant, tolerates alkaline soil, grows 6-10ft. Bloom in spring is intensely fragrant — major bee attractor. Excellent support species for nearby fruit trees.

Fast-growing cool-season green — 30-40 days from seed to harvest. Excellent bee forage when allowed to flower (white flowers attract beneficials). Self-seeds prolifically under fruit trees for a perpetual groundcover of edible greens in fall and spring. Bolts in heat — treat as seasonal groundcover.

Texas native elder better adapted to alkaline soils and heat than common elderberry. Grows to 20ft as a large shrub or small tree. Fragrant white flower clusters attract pollinators. Berries used for syrup and wildlife. More drought-tolerant than S. nigra. Natural in Central Texas limestone country.
toxicityWarning: Raw berries and uncooked plant parts can cause nausea — always cook berries before consuming

description: Extremely heat and drought-tolerant shrub producing superfoods berries. Native relatives (Lycium carolinianum) are present in Texas. Thorny arching canes make it a wildlife refuge. Berries are edible fresh or dried. Can be coppiced. Tolerates alkaline and poor soils — one of the most resilient shrubs for Texas conditions.

Premier bee forage plant — rated among the top plants for honey bee and butterfly attraction. Fragrant leaves used as herb. Self-seeds freely. Short-lived perennial (2-3 years) but perpetuates by self-seeding. Excellent insectary under fruit trees. Blooms July–September when other summer flowers are fading.

Excellent groundcover for Texas food forests in spring and fall. Everbearing varieties (Seascape, Ozark Beauty) produce fall through spring in Texas. Go dormant in summer heat. Plant under deciduous fruit trees where summer shade provides some cooling. Runners create a living groundcover carpet.

Texas native blackberries (Rubus trivialis) are extremely vigorous and productive. Thornless varieties (Arapaho, Navaho, Kiowa) are excellent for food forests. Plant on north or east slopes for partial shade from afternoon sun. Canes fruit in year two — remove old canes after harvest. Wildlife magnet.

Grape-sized kiwi fruits without the fuzzy skin. Much more cold-hardy than commercial kiwi. Vigorous vine reaching 20-30ft — needs strong trellis or large tree to climb. Requires male and female plants (1 male per 6-8 females). Produces abundantly once established (5-7 years). Long-lived (50+ years).
